content.claim: Early tracking increases educational inequality; Hungary exhibits large socioeconomic performance gaps (170-point PISA reading gap between most and least advantaged decile, equivalent to approximately four years of schooling).
content.context: Fresh live research through the Political feasibility lens
limitations: The causal attribution of Hungary's specific inequality gap directly to 6/8-year gimnázium tracks is not established in notes reviewed
PISA measures 15-year-olds and does not isolate the effect of early selection at age 10 from other stratifying factors
Source does not clarify whether 170-point gap is attributable solely to tracking or reflects broader structural inequalities
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
